definition: The pleasant, earthy smell produced when rain falls on dry soil.
etymology: Coined in 1964 from Greek 'petra' (stone) and 'ichor' (the fluid that flows in the veins of the gods in Greek mythology).
exampleSentence: After weeks of drought, the first drops of rain released a glorious petrichor across the garden.

definition: The realisation that each random passer-by is living a life as vivid and complex as your own.
etymology: Coined by John Koenig in 'The Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows' (2012), from German 'sonder' (special) and French 'sonder' (to probe).
exampleSentence: Standing in the busy station, she was struck by a wave of sonder as she watched hundreds of strangers rush past.

definition: The act of throwing someone or something out of a window.
etymology: From Latin 'de' (down from) and 'fenestra' (window). Popularised after the Defenestrations of Prague in 1419 and 1618.
exampleSentence: The defenestration of the laptop was perhaps an overreaction to the software update.

definition: An involuntary state of intense romantic desire and obsessive attachment to another person.
etymology: Coined by psychologist Dorothy Tennov in 1979, possibly derived from an arbitrary coinage designed to sound clinical.
exampleSentence: What he mistook for true love was, his therapist gently suggested, a textbook case of limerence.

definition: Relating to or given to the use of long words.
etymology: From Latin 'sesquipedalis', literally 'a foot and a half long', from 'sesqui' (one and a half) and 'pes' (foot). Used by Horace in Ars Poetica.
exampleSentence: His sesquipedalian prose made even simple recipes read like doctoral theses.

definition: The point in a planet's orbit where it is farthest from the sun.
etymology: From Greek 'apo' (away from) and 'helios' (sun). First used in English around 1656.
exampleSentence: Earth reaches its aphelion in early July, ironically during the Northern Hemisphere's summer.

definition: The strange wistfulness of used bookshops, which are somehow infused with the passage of time.
etymology: Coined by John Koenig in 'The Dictionary of Obscure Sorrows', blending 'vellum' (parchment) with a suffix echoing 'petrichor'.
exampleSentence: Browsing the dusty shelves of the antique shop, she was overcome by a deep vellichor.

definition: The dark grey colour seen by the eye in perfect darkness, as opposed to true black.
etymology: German for 'intrinsic grey' or 'own grey'. Documented in 19th-century vision research. Also known as 'Eigenlicht' (own light).
exampleSentence: Even in the cave with no light whatsoever, his visual field was filled not with black but with eigengrau.

definition: The separation of parts of a compound word by an intervening word, used for emphasis.
etymology: From Greek 'tmesis' (cutting), from 'temnein' (to cut). Used in rhetoric since antiquity.
exampleSentence: When she said 'abso-bloomin-lutely', she was employing a classic example of tmesis.

definition: The sensation of seeing light without light actually entering the eye, such as when you press on your closed eyelids.
etymology: From Greek 'phos' (light) and 'phainein' (to show). Coined in the 19th century by J.E. Purkinje.
exampleSentence: As a child, she loved pressing her palms against her eyes to watch the kaleidoscopic phosphenes bloom.

definition: The warmth of the sun in winter.
etymology: From Latin 'apricus' (exposed to the sun, sunny). An archaic English word recorded as early as 1623.
exampleSentence: They sat on the frozen bench, grateful for the unexpected apricity on that January morning.

definition: The sound of wind whispering through the trees.
etymology: From Greek 'psithuros' (whispering), related to 'psithurizo' (I whisper).
exampleSentence: The gentle psithurism of the birch trees made the forest feel alive with quiet conversation.

definition: Having beautifully shaped buttocks.
etymology: From Greek 'kallos' (beauty) and 'pyge' (buttocks). Inspired by the Venus Callipyge statue in Naples.
exampleSentence: The art history lecture took an entertaining turn during the discussion of the callipygian Venus.

definition: Unfamiliar, rare, strange, and yet marvellous.
etymology: From Old English 'seldcūþ', combining 'seldan' (seldom) and 'cūþ' (known). Fell out of common use after the 14th century.
exampleSentence: The aurora borealis was a selcouth sight for someone who had never left the tropics.

definition: An ache for distant places; a longing for far-off lands one has never visited.
etymology: German, from 'fern' (far) and 'weh' (pain, woe). The opposite of 'Heimweh' (homesickness).
exampleSentence: Scrolling through photos of Patagonia filled her with an almost painful fernweh.

definition: A mechanical model of the solar system showing the relative positions and motions of the planets.
etymology: Named after Charles Boyle, 4th Earl of Orrery, for whom one of the first such devices was made around 1713.
exampleSentence: The antique orrery in the museum still turned its brass planets in graceful arcs around a golden sun.

definition: An excessive desire to stay in bed; an obsessive attachment to one's bed.
etymology: From Greek 'klinein' (to lean, recline) and 'mania' (madness, obsession).
exampleSentence: On dark winter mornings, his clinomania made the alarm clock feel like a personal enemy.

definition: An alignment of three or more celestial bodies in a straight line, typically the sun, earth, and moon.
etymology: From Greek 'suzugia' (yoked together), from 'sun' (together) and 'zugon' (yoke). Also notable for having no standard vowels in English.
exampleSentence: The eclipse was caused by a perfect syzygy of the sun, moon, and earth.

definition: Obedient or attentive to an excessive or servile degree.
etymology: From Latin 'obsequiosus' (compliant), from 'obsequi' (to follow, comply with), from 'ob' (towards) and 'sequi' (to follow).
exampleSentence: The obsequious waiter hovered so persistently that they could barely hold a conversation.

definition: Lasting for a very short time; transient.
etymology: From Greek 'ephemeros' (lasting only a day), from 'epi' (on) and 'hemera' (day). Originally used in medicine for fevers lasting a single day.
exampleSentence: The cherry blossoms were a reminder that the most beautiful things are often the most ephemeral.

definition: Kept secret, especially because it would not be approved of.
etymology: From Latin 'surrepticius' (stolen, secret), from 'surripere' (to seize secretly), from 'sub' (under) and 'rapere' (to seize).
exampleSentence: She took a surreptitious glance at her phone under the table during the interminable meeting.

definition: Too great or extreme to be expressed or described in words.
etymology: From Latin 'ineffabilis', from 'in' (not) and 'effabilis' (utterable), from 'effari' (to speak out).
exampleSentence: The view from the summit filled her with an ineffable sense of wonder.

definition: Sweet or musical; pleasant to hear.
etymology: From Latin 'mellifluus', from 'mel' (honey) and 'fluere' (to flow). Literally 'flowing with honey'.
exampleSentence: The cellist's mellifluous playing brought several audience members to tears.

definition: Pleasure derived from another person's misfortune.
etymology: German, from 'Schaden' (damage, harm) and 'Freude' (joy). Borrowed into English in the 19th century, as no single English word captures the concept.
exampleSentence: He felt a twinge of schadenfreude watching his rival trip on stage, though he quickly felt guilty about it.

definition: A manuscript or piece of writing on which the original text has been erased and overwritten, yet traces of the original remain visible.
etymology: From Greek 'palimpsestos' (scraped again), from 'palin' (again) and 'psao' (to scrape). Parchment was expensive, so monks frequently scraped and reused it.
exampleSentence: The city itself was a palimpsest, each era's architecture layered atop the ruins of the last.

definition: Complaining in a petulant or whining manner.
etymology: From Latin 'querulus' (complaining), from 'queri' (to complain). Related to 'quarrel'.
exampleSentence: The querulous neighbour had filed complaints about everything from birdsong to the colour of their bins.

definition: To take a walk in the wind for fun; to go out in windy weather to clear one's head.
etymology: Dutch, from 'uit' (out) and 'waaien' (to blow). A concept deeply embedded in Dutch coastal culture.
exampleSentence: After a stressful week, she drove to the coast to uitwaaien along the cliffs.

definition: A state of serene calmness; freedom from mental disturbance or anxiety.
etymology: From Greek 'ataraxia' (impassiveness), from 'a' (without) and 'tarassein' (to disturb). A key concept in Epicurean and Stoic philosophy.
exampleSentence: Through years of meditation, she had achieved something approaching true ataraxia.

definition: An irresistible urge to do something inadvisable.
etymology: From Greek 'kakoethes' (ill-disposed), from 'kakos' (bad) and 'ethos' (character, habit). Used by Juvenal as 'cacoethes scribendi' (an incurable itch to write).
exampleSentence: He suffered from a cacoethes for correcting other people's grammar — a habit that won him few friends.

definition: The partially shaded outer region of a shadow cast by an opaque object; or a peripheral or indeterminate area.
etymology: From Latin 'paene' (almost) and 'umbra' (shadow). Coined by astronomer Johannes Kepler in 1604.
exampleSentence: The truth lay somewhere in the penumbra between what he said and what he actually meant.
